tweets.text:
🇫🇷 🇲🇨 Très heureux de partager ce portrait publié dans @LePoint (via @philippinerbt), qui revient sur mon parcours, ma méthode et ma vision de la macroéconomie.

Derrière les classements, les prévisions et les chiffres, il y a surtout une conviction profonde que la macroéconomie n’est pas un exercice de certitude mais plutôt un travail permanent de discipline, de probabilités et d’humilité.

Prévoir n’est pas chercher à avoir raison contre le marché mais plutôt essayer de comprendre plus vite ce qui change, d’identifier les signaux faibles, de distinguer le bruit de l’information utile, et d’accepter que chaque scénario doit être révisé dès que les données l’exigent.

Dans un monde où les cycles sont plus courts, les chocs plus fréquents et les réactions de marché parfois plus violentes, l’analyse macro doit rester à la fois rigoureuse, réactive et profondément connectée au réel à travers l'emploi, l'inflation, la politique monétaire, la consommation, le crédit, les flux et la géopolitique.

C’est cette approche que j’essaie de défendre au quotidien, celle d'une macroéconomie utile, opérationnelle, fondée sur les données, mais jamais déconnectée du terrain.

Merci au Point et surtout à Philippine Robert pour ce portrait, et à toutes celles et ceux qui suivent, challengent et enrichissent chaque jour mes analyses.

➡ Lien en commentaire:

tweets.text:
🇺🇸WSL ELECTION<GO> just got a big revamp on @TheTerminal for the US mid-term elections.

You can now track real-time @Kalshi prediction markets tick-by-tick across all major races — including individual Senate & House contests. 

It's all tied together by Kalshi's new American Power Index (KPOW), which distills all the House and Senate race markets into a single measure of where Congressional control stands.

The 2028 US general and Non-US election markets are also available in the worksheet — global coverage is just a tab away.
tweets.full_text:
🇺🇸WSL ELECTION<GO> just got a big revamp on @TheTerminal for the US mid-term elections.

You can now track real-time @Kalshi prediction markets tick-by-tick across all major races — including individual Senate & House contests. 

It's all tied together by Kalshi's new American Power Index (KPOW), which distills all the House and Senate race markets into a single measure of where Congressional control stands.

The 2028 US general and Non-US election markets are also available in the worksheet — global coverage is just a tab away.

tweets.text: $MU at the heart of this dilemma.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Historically, semis were priced as a cyclical industry. As margins peaked, P/S ratios would fall.

Over the past year, though, semi multiples have exploded as margins hit all-time highs.

Either the industry has become less cyclical OR it is a sign of exuberance. https://t.co/OX59m4DA1W
tweets.full_text: $MU at the heart of this dilemma.

thread_tweets.text:
The cure for high prices is high prices - eventually.

Demand will eventually fall in response to these price increases.

And when that happens, memory prices should go down.

Question is: what is the breaking point and how much more expensive will things get before the bust? https://t.co/zZZ84WcCyB

tweets.text: Good Morning from Germany, where consumer confidence remains stuck near crisis levels. GfK Consumer Sentiment for July edged up only slightly to -29.2 from -29.7, still far below pre-Covid levels. Income expectations improved a bit, but buying appetite stays weak and savings intentions high; bad news for Germany’s private consumption engine.

tweets.text:
That was a thud.

KOSPI just fell nearly 10% in one session. It is only the 15th such drop in more than 40 years.
After similar drops during strong uptrends that were made back within 3 sessions, KOSPI was higher one week later 93% of the time.

Longer term, 1997, 2000, and 2008 still led to significant losses.

Read full analysis: https://t.co/IxEHDUAior

tweets.text:
The US cannot inflate its way out of its fiscal problems. 

This is a myth. 

All the future obligations are index-linked to inflation. 

All you’ll do with this idea is widen the wealth divide between the recipients of the inflation-indexed payments and wage earners.

tweets.text:
In the last 4 quarters, Cyclical GDP is up $103 billion (~2% growth).

Information processing equipment is up $148 billion.

Cyclical GDP is expanding at a healthy pace, but over 100% of the growth is from computer equipment.

tweets.text: All these forecasts of memory spending seem to assume that behaviour will not change much in response to higher prices.
tweets.full_text: All these forecasts of memory spending seem to assume that behaviour will not change much in response to higher prices.

tweets.text: It’s interesting how different parts of the AI supply chain have reacted differently to shortages. For companies making turbines, it’s like “sorry we’re sold out, check back in five years”. For memory, it’s “yes, we have what you need but we will charge you 1000% more.”
tweets.full_text: It’s interesting how different parts of the AI supply chain have reacted differently to shortages. For companies making turbines, it’s like “sorry we’re sold out, check back in five years”. For memory, it’s “yes, we have what you need but we will charge you 1000% more.”

tweets.text: There used to be a time when flying where if you forgot to put your phone in airplane mode, it would hook up to some crappy satellite service that would charge huge amounts of money for a tiny bit of data. I wonder how much AI spending is like that now.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: NEW: Fintech startup Slash reveals it was forced to roll back its AI coding push after an employee burned $80,000 in tokens in the first week.
